## Atoms.dev — Competitive Brief (2026-06-01)

**Run date:** 2026-06-01 | **Window:** 2026-05-25 → 2026-06-01 | **Baseline:** DEV-62 (2026-05-26)

---

### What It Builds
Full-stack web apps and SaaS products from a natural-language prompt, using a simulated team of 8 named AI agents (Iris: research; Emma: PM; Bob: architecture; Alex: engineering; Mike: lead; Sarah: SEO; David: data; Adrian: ads). Ships with auth, PostgreSQL, Stripe, SEO, and one-click deploy. Race Mode (Max plan): four AI models build simultaneously; user picks the best result. No material product change this week; blog has had no posts since May 5, 2026.

### Onboarding Flow
Prompt → Iris does pre-build market research → 8-agent team builds concurrently → one-click deploy to Atoms Cloud. Race Mode (Max): four model builds run simultaneously. No changelog or /whats-new surface (/changelog and /whats-new both 404 as of 2026-06-01).

### Pricing (2026-06-01)
| Plan | Price | Credits/mo | Storage | Notes |
|------|-------|------------|---------|-------|
| Free | $0 | 15/day (25/mo) | 2 GB | 2 Cloud projects |
| Pro | $20/mo | 100 | 10 GB | Private projects, custom domains |
| Max | $100/mo | 500 | 100 GB | Race Mode, higher accuracy |

Team Plan (Apr 2026) absent from public pricing page — likely still in limited beta.

### Strengths
1. **Named-agent UX transparency** — 8 visible specialist agents with defined roles create user trust: "a real team is working on this." No competitor shows this. [Source: atoms.dev homepage]
2. **Pre-build research gate (Iris)** — market research, competitive analysis, and audience sizing before any code runs. 4/6 competitors now ship some form of this. [Source: atoms.dev/blog]
3. **Race Mode** — 4 parallel model iterations; user picks best result. Quality multiplier unavailable in any other platform. [Source: atoms.dev/blog]

### Weaknesses
1. **No public changelog** — /changelog and /whats-new both 404; opacity about what changed makes trust-building difficult.
2. **Race Mode gated at $100/mo** — the quality multiplier is locked behind Max tier; Pro users get no parallel-model benefit.
3. **No Arabic/RTL support found** — zero evidence of MENA market consideration.

### Patterns to Copy
- **Named-agent UX** — surface agent roles (researcher, PM, architect, engineer, QA) visibly in our build interface; builds trust without adding friction.
- **Pre-build research gate** — before starting a build, run a lightweight market/competitive research step (what exists, who uses it, what differentiates it). Iris pattern.

### Patterns to Beat
- **Arabic/RTL gap** — Atoms ships no Arabic/RTL support; our MENA-native defaults are a structural competitive advantage in the Kuwait/GCC market.
- **Changelog opacity** — our transparent, queryable issue/comment history is a trust differentiator vs Atoms' 404 changelog.

### Decision
**Study only** — Named-agent UX and pre-build research gate are worth absorbing as prompt/UX patterns; no import or rebuild warranted. Race Mode is architecturally interesting but the $100 price point and closed API limit practical adoption.

### Sources
- https://atoms.dev
- https://atoms.dev/blog (last post May 5, 2026)
- https://github.com/FoundationAgents/MetaGPT
- https://toolworthy.ai (Atoms.dev analysis)

### Diff vs 2026-05-26 Baseline
- **new_features:** No material new features this week (no blog posts since May 5)
- **pricing_changes:** —
- **new_flows:** —
- **removed:** —
- **positioning:** Hero headline changed from "world's first AI software company" (MGX era) to "Turn ideas into products that sell / AI employees to validate ideas, build products, and acquire customers" — pivoting from tech identity to founder/operator GTM; 8th agent (David, Data Analyst) now explicitly listed on homepage separately from Adrian

## Antigravity 2.0 (Google) — Competitive Brief (2026-06-01)

**Run date:** 2026-06-01 | **Window:** 2026-05-25 → 2026-06-01 | **Baseline:** DEV-62 (2026-05-26, just 7 days after GA launch)

---

### What It Builds
Google's agentic developer platform — a VS Code-forked desktop IDE plus CLI, SDK, and Managed Agents API. Handles full-stack web apps, long-running automated workflows (dependency upgrades, security scans, multi-repo refactoring), and agentic CI/CD pipelines. Dynamic subagents run frontend/backend/testing in parallel; a browser subagent (live Chrome instance) visually verifies generated UI without manual DevTools. This week: post-launch stabilization (v2.0.6, v2.0.10 patches), Linux sandboxing extended, IDE integration button added.

### Onboarding Flow
Install Antigravity desktop app (macOS/Linux/Windows) → connect GitHub repo or Google Cloud workspace → prompt agent. Manager Surface: observe multiple agents across workspaces from one interface. Scheduled tasks: cron-based background agents for dependency scans, security sweeps. IDE integration button (v2.0.6): one-click open of current workspace in Antigravity IDE.

### Pricing (2026-06-01)
| Plan | Price | Notes |
|------|-------|-------|
| Free (Individuals) | $0 | 20 req/day (slashed from 250); full feature access |
| AI Pro (bundled) | ~$20/mo | Google AI Pro subscription |
| AI Ultra | $100/mo | 5× Pro limits |
| AI Ultra Premium | $200/mo | 20× Pro limits (was $250) |
| Enterprise | Custom | VPC, SSO, audit logs, BigQuery |

**Key change:** Free tier slashed from 250 → 20 req/day. $100 bonus credits promo expired May 25.

### Strengths
1. **Parallel dynamic subagents** — frontend/backend/testing agents run concurrently by default; A2A protocol enables inter-agent communication. Only platform in cohort to architect this at the OS level. [Source: google.com/antigravity, Google I/O 2026]
2. **Scheduled cron agents** — agents run background jobs on schedule (dependency scans, security sweeps) without user prompts. No competitor ships this as a first-class feature. [Source: Antigravity 2.0 docs]
3. **Google ecosystem depth** — native Firebase, Android, AI Studio, Google Cloud integrations; enterprise VPC/SSO/audit via Managed Agents API. [Source: Google I/O 2026 blog]

### Weaknesses
1. **Free tier gutted** — 250 → 20 req/day is a 92% reduction; makes experimentation impractical without paid plan.
2. **Post-launch stability** — v2.0.2–v2.0.9 were emergency patches (config wipes, data loss, SSH/WSL breakage from automatic upgrade); trust damage is real.
3. **No built-in deploy** — requires existing infra; contrast Lovable/Bolt one-click deploy.

### Patterns to Copy
- **Scheduled cron agents** — agents run on schedule for routine maintenance (dependency checks, security scans, changelog summaries). Directly applicable to our weekly sweep autopilot and QA automation.
- **A2A inter-agent protocol** — typed message contracts between agents enable handoff without free-text context passing.

### Patterns to Beat
- **Free tier gutted** — our accessible pricing (or volume-based model) is a competitive wedge for individual developers and small teams.
- **Desktop-only** — our browser-accessible agents vs Antigravity's install-required approach.

### Decision
**Study only** — Parallel subagent orchestration and scheduled cron agent patterns are architecturally worth studying; A2A protocol is directly applicable to our squad handoff model. Do not recommend Antigravity to clients until MCP auth fragmentation and the slashed free tier are addressed.

### Sources
- https://antigravity.google (or current URL)
- https://developers.google.com/antigravity (changelog)
- https://techcrunch.com (Google I/O 2026 coverage, May 19 2026)
- https://gradually.ai (Antigravity changelog tracker)

### Diff vs 2026-05-26 Baseline
- **new_features:** v2.0.6 IDE integration button (one-click open workspace in Antigravity IDE, May 22); v2.0.10 G1 credit bug fix + reliability (May 28); Linux sandboxing extended (previously macOS-only)
- **pricing_changes:** Free tier slashed 250 → 20 req/day; $100 bonus credits promo expired May 25
- **new_flows:** IDE integration button reduces context-switch friction
- **removed:** —
- **positioning:** Unchanged (professional developer platform, Google ecosystem depth)

## Replit Agent — Competitive Brief (2026-06-01)

**Run date:** 2026-06-01 | **Window:** 2026-05-25 → 2026-06-01 | **Baseline:** DEV-62 (2026-05-26)

---

### What It Builds
Full-stack web and mobile apps from natural language in a cloud IDE, with autonomous 200-min runs and real-browser self-testing. Agent 4 (2026) runs parallel artifact builds. This week shipped five new generation modalities: audio (music/SFX/speech), 3D models (Tripo3D), and Canvas-native media (images, vectors, videos). Also added Stripe one-click install from Marketplace (no API key copying required). Pivoting hard toward enterprise: Visa investment, $9B valuation, Solution Partner Program launched.

### Onboarding Flow
Prompt → live URL in <2 minutes. Plan-while-build mode (Agent 4): agent shows execution plan concurrently as it builds. Stripe Marketplace (May 29): one-click install, no API key copying. Design Canvas: Figma import → static site in <2 min. 30+ connectors platform.

### Pricing (2026-06-01)
| Plan | Price | Notes |
|------|-------|-------|
| Starter | $0 | Limited |
| Core | $20/mo (annual) | $20 credits included |
| Pro | $95/mo (annual) | 15 builders |
| Enterprise | Custom | SLA, SSO |

No changes from May 26 baseline.

### Strengths
1. **Five new generation modalities (May 29)** — Agent audio generation (music/SFX/speech), Tripo3D connector (3D from text/image), Canvas media generation (images/vectors/videos), Stripe one-click Marketplace install, Enterprise self-serve up to $200K. [Source: blog.replit.com, May 29 2026]
2. **Enterprise pivot signals** — Visa investment (May 28), Solution Partner Program (Accenture, Slalom, Hexaware); valuation $3B (Sep 2025) → $9B (Mar 2026); fastest-growing enterprise trajectory in this cohort. [Source: blog.replit.com]
3. **Connector-as-app-install pattern (Stripe Marketplace)** — one-click install eliminates API key copying friction; immediately applicable to any connector integration. [Source: blog.replit.com, May 29]

### Weaknesses
1. **Credit opacity** — effort-based pricing model; large builds are unpredictable in cost.
2. **Cloud-only** — no local/offline mode; existing repos with complex Git workflows are awkward.
3. **Plan-while-build vs plan-before-build** — shows plan concurrently but doesn't enforce approval gate before code runs.

### Patterns to Copy
- **One-click connector install (Marketplace)** — connectors install as apps with no API key copying; user grants permissions, not credentials. Directly applicable to our integration scaffolding.
- **Plan-while-build** — show the agent's execution plan to the user in real-time as the build proceeds, not as a blocking gate.
- **Audio/3D generation agents** — media-type-specific agents as first-class build outputs (not post-build steps).

### Patterns to Beat
- **Credit opacity** — our task-bounded credit model (transparent cost per task type) is a competitive wedge.
- **Cloud-only** — our repo-native stack runs anywhere; Replit requires cloud.

### Decision
**Adapt** — The connector-as-app-install pattern (Stripe Marketplace one-click) is immediately importable as an integration scaffolding improvement (S effort, high impact for client-facing app builds). Plan-while-build is worth adding to our build agent UX.

### Sources
- https://blog.replit.com (May 29, May 28 2026 posts)
- https://replit.com/pricing
- https://docs.replit.com/updates

### Diff vs 2026-05-26 Baseline
- **new_features:** Agent audio generation, Tripo3D connector, Canvas media generation (images/vectors/videos), Stripe one-click Marketplace install, Enterprise self-serve up to $200K (all May 29); Visa investment + Intelligent Commerce integration exploratory (May 28)
- **pricing_changes:** —
- **new_flows:** Stripe Marketplace one-click install (no API key copying); media generation modalities in Canvas
- **removed:** —
- **positioning:** Accelerating pivot from "AI coding playground" to "enterprise AI software factory"; Solution Partner Program with Accenture/Slalom/Hexaware signals enterprise GTM

## Cursor (agent mode) — Competitive Brief (2026-06-01)

**Run date:** 2026-06-01 | **Window:** 2026-05-25 → 2026-06-01 | **Baseline:** DEV-62 (2026-05-26)

---

### What It Builds
An AI-native IDE (VS Code fork) with full-codebase agent mode for developers. Agents autonomously edit files across multiple repos, run terminal commands, browse the web, and visually verify UI via Computer Use. Background cloud agents process GitHub issues and Slack messages into draft PRs with no laptop needed. v3.6 (May 29) adds Auto-review Run Mode — a classifier subagent that triages Shell/MCP/Fetch calls into allowlist / sandbox / human-escalation buckets before execution.

### Onboarding Flow
Install Cursor IDE → connect GitHub repo → prompt agent. Background/Cloud Agents: input a GitHub issue or Slack message, receive a draft PR. Auto-review Run Mode: enabled per-project, classifier subagent runs before any Shell/MCP/Fetch call. Microsoft Teams: @Cursor in any Teams channel to delegate to a cloud agent (May 11).

### Pricing (2026-06-01)
| Plan | Price | Notes |
|------|-------|-------|
| Hobby (Free) | $0 | Basic completions |
| Pro | $20/mo | Full agent access |
| Pro+ | $60/mo | Higher limits |
| Ultra | $200/mo | Highest limits |
| Teams | $40/user/mo | Team features |

Base tier pricing unchanged. BugBot pricing changed: **from $40/seat/month flat → usage-based ~$1–1.50/run**, effective June 8, 2026. Configurable effort levels added.

### Strengths
1. **Auto-review Run Mode v3.6 (May 29)** — Classifier subagent triages Shell/MCP/Fetch calls into allowlist/sandbox/human-escalation before execution. First safety layer for agentic code execution in an IDE. [Source: cursor.com/changelog]
2. **Parallel /multitask + BugBot** — Spawns parallel async subagents per independent task branch; BugBot auto-reviews and fixes ~80% of PR issues autonomously. [Source: cursor.com/blog]
3. **Microsoft Teams integration (May 11)** — @Cursor in any Teams channel delegates to cloud agent; enterprise distribution play. [Source: cursor.com/changelog]

### Weaknesses
1. **Context window gap** — Effective context 70–120K (not marketed 200K); large repos hit limits on complex multi-file changes.
2. **IDE lock-in** — VS Code fork limits portability; not all JetBrains/Vim users will switch.
3. **Agent cost premium** — Runs cost 12–80% more than CLI alternatives at scale.

### Patterns to Copy
- **Classifier-gated autonomous execution** — before any Shell/MCP/Fetch call, a lightweight classifier subagent triages: allowlist (execute immediately) / sandbox (execute with restrictions) / human-escalation. Directly applicable to our build agents.
- **Effort-level configurability** — let users choose how deeply agents investigate before acting (fast/balanced/thorough), not just model selection.

### Patterns to Beat
- **IDE lock-in** — our agents work in any repo/environment; Cursor requires their specific IDE.
- **Funding-stage pricing risk** — $2B raise at $50B+ valuation means pricing pressure is coming; our cost model should stay transparent.

### Decision
**Study only** — Auto-review classifier pattern (triage before execution) is the most architecturally sound idea from this brief and directly importable as a prompt/rule layer in our build agents. BugBot's usage-based pricing change is worth monitoring as a signal.

### Sources
- https://www.cursor.com/changelog
- https://cursor.com/blog/cursor-3
- https://cursor.com/pricing

### Diff vs 2026-05-26 Baseline
- **new_features:** v3.6 Auto-review Run Mode (May 29) — classifier subagent triages Shell/MCP/Fetch calls before execution
- **pricing_changes:** BugBot changed from $40/seat/month → usage-based ~$1–1.50/run, effective June 8, 2026
- **new_flows:** Auto-review pre-execution triage; Microsoft Teams integration for cloud agents (May 11)
- **removed:** —
- **positioning:** "AI code editor" → "the best coding agent"; framed as "unified workspace for building software with agents"

## Bolt.new — Competitive Brief (2026-06-01)

**Run date:** 2026-06-01 | **Window:** 2026-05-25 → 2026-06-01 | **Baseline:** DEV-62 (2026-05-26)

---

### What It Builds
React/Vite/TypeScript web apps generated entirely in-browser via StackBlitz WebContainers, with Expo-based iOS/Android from a single prompt. The AI has full filesystem, Node server, package manager, terminal, and browser console control. Design System Agents (May 29) now let teams ingest their own design system (npm package, Storybook, or repo) so Bolt generates production-fidelity code using real tokens and real components instead of hallucinated elements.

### Onboarding Flow
Zero-install browser session → prompt → live app. Dual agent system: Standard (Sonnet 4.6, balanced) for routine builds; Max (Opus 4.6, deep reasoning) for complex projects. Design System Agents activated by connecting npm package/Storybook/repo. Real-time multi-user collaboration live since Apr 2026.

### Pricing (2026-06-01)
| Plan | Price | Tokens/mo | Notes |
|------|-------|-----------|-------|
| Free | $0 | 1M | Bolt branding on sites |
| Pro | $25/mo | 10M+ | Token rollover, custom domains, AI image edit |
| Teams | $30/member/mo | 10M+/member | Private NPM, Design Systems, admin controls |
| Enterprise | Custom | Custom | SSO, audit logs, SLAs |

No changes from May 26 baseline. "MaxPro" appearing in UI — likely internal beta label, not a published tier.

### Strengths
1. **Design System Agents (May 29)** — Ingest team's npm package, Storybook, or repo; Bolt generates using real design tokens and real components. Closes the "Bolt generates ugly generic UIs" complaint. [Source: bolt.new blog, May 29 2026]
2. **OSS surface** — `stackblitz-labs/bolt.diy` (MIT, 15+ LLM providers, 19K+ stars) enables self-hosted/custom-model deployments — no competitor has this. [Source: github.com/stackblitz-labs/bolt.diy]
3. **Zero-install + in-browser** — No local setup, no VM spin-up latency; entire stack runs in WebContainer. [Source: bolt.new]

### Weaknesses
1. **Token wall** — High token burn on complex apps; free tier exhausts quickly; v1 hard sunset Aug 3 2026 creates migration trust gap.
2. **Positioning drift** — "98% less errors" and "1,000x larger projects" claims without published benchmarks undermine credibility.
3. **Deployment gap** — Bolt V2 Cloud is newer; enterprise teams may still need external infra.

### Patterns to Copy
- **Design system ingest** — accept a team's npm package or Storybook and use real tokens/components in generated code instead of generic defaults.
- **Dual-agent routing** — route routine tasks to a fast/cheap model (Sonnet 4.6) and complex tasks to a deep-reasoning model (Opus 4.6) automatically, not manually.

### Patterns to Beat
- **v1 hard sunset (Aug 3, 2026)** — forced migrations erode trust; our rolling skill-upgrade approach keeps backward compatibility.
- **Token opacity** — users don't know how many tokens a build will cost; our credit model should be transparent and bounded per task type.

### Decision
**Adapt** — Design System Agents and dual-agent routing are worth implementing. Design system ingest is a high-impact, low-effort skill (S effort, direct win-rate improvement for design-led clients). The v1 migration trust failure is an exploitable competitive gap.

### Sources
- https://bolt.new
- https://bolt.new/blog (Design System Agents, May 29 2026)
- https://github.com/stackblitz-labs/bolt.diy
- https://bolt.new/pricing

### Diff vs 2026-05-26 Baseline
- **new_features:** Design System Agents (May 29) — ingest npm/Storybook/repo for production-fidelity code generation
- **pricing_changes:** —
- **new_flows:** Design System Agent onboarding (connect design system before build)
- **removed:** "Open in StackBlitz" export no longer available for new projects
- **positioning:** Speed-first → reliability/scale ("98% less errors," "1,000x larger projects"); "MaxPro" internal beta label appearing in UI

## Lovable — Competitive Brief (2026-06-01)

**Run date:** 2026-06-01 | **Window:** 2026-05-25 → 2026-06-01 | **Baseline:** DEV-62 (2026-05-26)

---

### What It Builds
Full-stack web apps (React/TypeScript + Supabase) from a chat prompt, with TanStack Start + SSR as the default stack since May 13. Ships mobile wrappers (iOS/Android), SaaS dashboards, Shopify storefronts, and internal tools. Now adds autonomous helper subagents (May 27) that run parallel read-only investigations alongside the main build agent — the first vibe-coding competitor to ship this architecture.

### Onboarding Flow
Prompt → live Supabase-backed app with custom domain in minutes. Plan Mode (Feb 2026) shows approach before code runs. Subagents (May 27) augment the main agent with parallel codebase research and code review automatically.

### Pricing (2026-06-01)
| Plan | Price | Credits/mo | Notes |
|------|-------|------------|-------|
| Free | $0 | 30/mo (5/day) | Public projects only |
| Pro | $25/mo | 100 | Private projects, custom domains |
| Business | $50/mo | ~300 | SSO, branded URLs, audit logs, Wiz scanning |
| Enterprise | Custom | Custom | SCIM, dedicated support, custom API |

No changes from May 26 baseline.

### Strengths
1. **Subagents (May 27)** — Parallel autonomous helper agents run read-only investigation tasks (codebase research, code review, data synthesis) concurrently with the main build agent. First competitor to ship this. Available all plans. [Source: lovable.dev/blog]
2. **MCP + connector ecosystem (50+)** — Custom MCP confirmed on all plans (including Free) as of May 25. Algolia, LinkedIn, SharePoint connectors added May 28. [Source: docs.lovable.dev/changelog]
3. **Security posture** — Enterprise PII scanning with chat send protection (May 27); Wiz scanning; SCIM; audit logs; security center. [Source: lovable.dev/changelog]

### Weaknesses
1. **RedAccess security incident** — ~5,000 apps exposed sensitive corporate data due to default-public publish setting. A structural product flaw, not just a user error.
2. **No true autonomous build loop** — Subagents handle investigation, not code execution. Every significant edit still requires a user prompt.
3. **React/TypeScript only** — No Vue, Svelte, plain HTML, or native mobile (React Native/Expo).

### Patterns to Copy
- **Parallel investigation subagents** — spawn read-only analysis agents (codebase research, review, synthesis) alongside the main build agent to reduce context switches and accelerate planning.
- **MCP on all plans** — remove MCP as a paid gate; every user gets tool extensibility as a default.

### Patterns to Beat
- **Default-public apps** — Lovable's RedAccess incident (5,000 exposed apps) is a direct competitive opportunity: ship private-by-default publish flow and position it explicitly in messaging.
- **React-only lock-in** — our stack-agnostic scaffolding is a differentiator for clients with existing Vue/Svelte codebases.

### Decision
**Study only** — The subagents architecture (parallel investigation agents) is the architecturally differentiated move of the week and worth studying, but requires orchestration-layer work to adapt (4–6 week horizon). The most immediately actionable opportunity is positioning our private-by-default publish flow against Lovable's RedAccess incident.

### Sources
- https://lovable.dev/changelog
- https://docs.lovable.dev/changelog
- https://lovable.dev/pricing
- https://lovable.dev/blog (subagents announcement, May 27)

### Diff vs 2026-05-26 Baseline
- **new_features:** Subagents (May 27), Algolia/LinkedIn/SharePoint connectors (May 28), GPT Image 2 + GPT Image 1 Mini in AI connector (May 27), Enterprise PII scanning (May 27), Workspace delete with 60-day grace (May 26)
- **pricing_changes:** —
- **new_flows:** Subagent-augmented build session (investigation runs in parallel automatically)
- **removed:** —
- **positioning:** Unchanged ("Build something Lovable")

issues.description:
Daily Command Center Digest. Mode=create_issue. Runs every day 07:00 Asia/Kuwait (GMT+3). Produces ONE per-project status briefing comment on the dated issue this autopilot creates, so the CEO + workspace owner can see — at a glance — every project's state and what to push today.

CRITICAL SAFETY RULES:
- Mode is create_issue. Do not create additional issues. Do not change status on any other issue.
- NEVER mention any agent or member in the digest comment. Owner reads the dated issue directly; mentions would loop (Factory Pulse no-mention rule).
- NEVER touch DEV-36 (workspace-operations dialog) — exclude it from every list, count, and section.
- Quiet on green days: if NOTHING actionable changed since yesterday's digest (no new blockers, no stale in_review, no new approval ask, no project lifecycle_phase change, no new app-request), do NOT post any comment. Leave the auto-created issue body empty so it can be auto-cancelled by step (8).
- Use `--output json` on every CLI call. Continue on single-call failure (log to stdout and skip that signal).

STEPS:

(1) Snapshot the workspace.
    - `multica project list --output json` → all projects.
    - `multica issue list --output json` → all issues (paged if needed).
    - For each non-cancelled issue: record id, identifier, title, status, project_id, parent_issue_id, assignee_id, assignee_type, updated_at.
    - Exclude DEV-36 (id e60d5be5-b9dd-48aa-b949-28286ea80f1b) everywhere.
    - Build agent + squad name maps once: `multica agent list --output json` and `multica squad list --output json`. Used for assignee names; never for mention links.

(2) Detect "since yesterday" deltas. Treat `since = (now - 24h)` UTC.
    - new_blockers: issues that flipped to status=blocked in the window (updated_at >= since AND status=blocked).
    - cleared_blockers: issues that left blocked in the window (status != blocked AND prior blocked_reason metadata key present — best-effort).
    - new_approval_asks: any issue whose metadata gained `waiting_on=owner` or `waiting_on=admin` in the window, or whose body/comments contain a fresh "Approval needed" line written in the window (cheap heuristic: latest comment created_at >= since and contains "approval needed" case-insensitive).
    - stale_in_review_48h: any issue status=in_review with updated_at older than 48h.
    - lifecycle_phase changes: any parent issue whose metadata `lifecycle_phase` changed (compare to value from yesterday's pin — see step 7).
    - new_app_requests: any issue created_at >= since in project `01 - App Requests` (f3089a97-c608-4599-913c-f331335e1abe).
    If ALL six lists are empty AND there is no stale in_review and no current blocked anywhere → green day. Skip to step (8) without posting.

(3) Per-project section assembly. For each project (sorted by project title prefix `00 -`, `01 -`, … and then APP projects):
    - Skip the project entirely if it has zero open issues (status in {todo, in_progress, in_review, blocked}) AND no new_app_requests AND no lifecycle change.
    - Goal: 1 sentence from project.description (truncate to ~140 chars at the first sentence boundary).
    - lifecycle_phase: read from the project's "anchor" parent issue (heuristic: the parent issue with the most children in this project; if APP project, the parent whose title starts with "APP — "). Show the value of metadata `lifecycle_phase` or "—" if unset.
    - Active issues: counts by status — `todo=N in_progress=N in_review=N blocked=N` (exclude DEV-36).
    - Blockers + waiting_on: list every issue in this project with status=blocked OR metadata `waiting_on` set. Format: `DEV-NN <title> — waiting_on=<value> | blocked_reason=<value>`. Cap 5 per project; "+N more" if truncated.
    - Next action + owner: the single highest-priority issue in this project that is `todo` or `in_progress` and not blocked. Format: `DEV-NN <title> — owner=<agent-or-squad name>` (plain name, NO mention link).
    - Risk: 1 line — pick the most material risk: stale in_review item, oldest blocker, or "WIP imbalance: <name> has N in_progress" if any agent in this project has >=4 open issues. If nothing material, write "Risk: low — no stale or overloaded items.".
    - Approval needed: yes/no. If yes, quote the exact ask (one sentence from the requesting issue's body/comment). Format: `Approval needed: yes — DEV-NN: "<exact ask>"` or `Approval needed: no`.

issues.description:
Daily Command Center Digest. Mode=create_issue. Runs every day 07:00 Asia/Kuwait (GMT+3). Produces ONE per-project status briefing comment on the dated issue this autopilot creates, so the CEO + workspace owner can see — at a glance — every project's state and what to push today.

CRITICAL SAFETY RULES:
- Mode is create_issue. Do not create additional issues. Do not change status on any other issue.
- NEVER mention any agent or member in the digest comment. Owner reads the dated issue directly; mentions would loop (Factory Pulse no-mention rule).
- NEVER touch DEV-36 (workspace-operations dialog) — exclude it from every list, count, and section.
- Quiet on green days: if NOTHING actionable changed since yesterday's digest (no new blockers, no stale in_review, no new approval ask, no project lifecycle_phase change, no new app-request), do NOT post any comment. Leave the auto-created issue body empty so it can be auto-cancelled by step (8).
- Use `--output json` on every CLI call. Continue on single-call failure (log to stdout and skip that signal).

STEPS:

(1) Snapshot the workspace.
    - `multica project list --output json` → all projects.
    - `multica issue list --output json` → all issues (paged if needed).
    - For each non-cancelled issue: record id, identifier, title, status, project_id, parent_issue_id, assignee_id, assignee_type, updated_at.
    - Exclude DEV-36 (id e60d5be5-b9dd-48aa-b949-28286ea80f1b) everywhere.
    - Build agent + squad name maps once: `multica agent list --output json` and `multica squad list --output json`. Used for assignee names; never for mention links.

(2) Detect "since yesterday" deltas. Treat `since = (now - 24h)` UTC.
    - new_blockers: issues that flipped to status=blocked in the window (updated_at >= since AND status=blocked).
    - cleared_blockers: issues that left blocked in the window (status != blocked AND prior blocked_reason metadata key present — best-effort).
    - new_approval_asks: any issue whose metadata gained `waiting_on=owner` or `waiting_on=admin` in the window, or whose body/comments contain a fresh "Approval needed" line written in the window (cheap heuristic: latest comment created_at >= since and contains "approval needed" case-insensitive).
    - stale_in_review_48h: any issue status=in_review with updated_at older than 48h.
    - lifecycle_phase changes: any parent issue whose metadata `lifecycle_phase` changed (compare to value from yesterday's pin — see step 7).
    - new_app_requests: any issue created_at >= since in project `01 - App Requests` (f3089a97-c608-4599-913c-f331335e1abe).
    If ALL six lists are empty AND there is no stale in_review and no current blocked anywhere → green day. Skip to step (8) without posting.

(3) Per-project section assembly. For each project (sorted by project title prefix `00 -`, `01 -`, … and then APP projects):
    - Skip the project entirely if it has zero open issues (status in {todo, in_progress, in_review, blocked}) AND no new_app_requests AND no lifecycle change.
    - Goal: 1 sentence from project.description (truncate to ~140 chars at the first sentence boundary).
    - lifecycle_phase: read from the project's "anchor" parent issue (heuristic: the parent issue with the most children in this project; if APP project, the parent whose title starts with "APP — "). Show the value of metadata `lifecycle_phase` or "—" if unset.
    - Active issues: counts by status — `todo=N in_progress=N in_review=N blocked=N` (exclude DEV-36).
    - Blockers + waiting_on: list every issue in this project with status=blocked OR metadata `waiting_on` set. Format: `DEV-NN <title> — waiting_on=<value> | blocked_reason=<value>`. Cap 5 per project; "+N more" if truncated.
    - Next action + owner: the single highest-priority issue in this project that is `todo` or `in_progress` and not blocked. Format: `DEV-NN <title> — owner=<agent-or-squad name>` (plain name, NO mention link).
    - Risk: 1 line — pick the most material risk: stale in_review item, oldest blocker, or "WIP imbalance: <name> has N in_progress" if any agent in this project has >=4 open issues. If nothing material, write "Risk: low — no stale or overloaded items.".
    - Approval needed: yes/no. If yes, quote the exact ask (one sentence from the requesting issue's body/comment). Format: `Approval needed: yes — DEV-NN: "<exact ask>"` or `Approval needed: no`.

(4) Compose the Top-3 block. Score every open issue across the workspace by:
    - urgency: blocked=4, stale_in_review_48h=3, has approval ask=3, deploy_gate=red=4, priority=urgent=4 / high=3 / medium=2 / low=1.
    - momentum: in_progress=2, in_review=1, todo=1, backlog=0.
    - age penalty: +1 per 24h since updated_at, cap +3.
    Final score = urgency + momentum + age_penalty. Pick the top 3 distinct issues (no two from the same parent unless nothing else qualifies).

(5) Render the digest comment using this exact shape (parseable by humans and future audits):

```
# Daily Command Center Digest — <YYYY-MM-DD KW>

**Window:** <since> → <now> (UTC) | **Projects scanned:** N | **Open issues:** N | **Blocked:** N | **Stale in_review (48h):** N | **Approval asks:** N

## Per-Project Status

### <project icon> <project title>
- **Goal:** <one sentence>
- **Phase:** <lifecycle_phase>
- **Active:** todo=<n> in_progress=<n> in_review=<n> blocked=<n>
- **Blockers / waiting_on:** <list or "none">
- **Next action:** <DEV-NN title — owner=name>
- **Risk:** <one line>
- **Approval needed:** <yes/no> [— "<exact ask>"]

### <next project>
… (same shape)

## Top 3 to Push Today

1. **DEV-NN** <title> — assignee=<name> | why: <one phrase>
2. **DEV-NN** <title> — assignee=<name> | why: <one phrase>
3. **DEV-NN** <title> — assignee=<name> | why: <one phrase>

## Deltas Since Yesterday
- New blockers: <DEV-NN list or "none">
- Cleared blockers: <DEV-NN list or "none">
- New approval asks: <DEV-NN list or "none">
- Stale in_review (48h+): <DEV-NN list or "none">
- New app requests: <DEV-NN list or "none">
- Lifecycle phase changes: <project: old→new or "none">
```

Post via: `multica issue comment add <this-issue-id> --content-stdin` (HEREDOC the body). NEVER include any `mention://agent/...` or `mention://member/...` link. Issue references should be plain `DEV-NN` text (no `mention://issue/...` either — keep the digest scannable).

(6) On a green day (step 2 short-circuited), do NOT post any comment. Skip to step (8).

(7) Pin metadata on THIS issue only if a value materially helps tomorrow's run:
    - `last_run_verdict` = "green" or "posted" (overwrite each run).
    - `last_window_end` = <RFC3339-now> (overwrite each run).
    - `lifecycle_phase_snapshot` = JSON map `{project_id: phase}` so step (2) can detect phase changes tomorrow.
    - `top3_count` = number (expect 0 on green days, 3 otherwise).
    Use `multica issue metadata set` once per changed key. Skip any key whose value is identical to what `metadata list` already shows.

(8) Status finalization.
    - If a digest was posted: flip THIS issue to `in_review` (`multica issue status <this-issue-id> in_review`). CEO + owner read it, then close.
    - If green day (no digest posted): flip THIS issue to `cancelled` with no comment so it stops cluttering the inbox.

(9) Output one line to stdout (only non-comment output): `Daily Digest @ <RFC3339> | verdict=<green|posted> | projects=N | top3=N | blocked=B stale=S approvals=A`.

OPERATING RULES:
- Never block on a single CLI failure. Log "skipped <signal>: <reason>" and continue with the remaining signals.
- All comments go ONLY on the issue this autopilot creates. Never comment on any other issue.
- Never mention any agent or member anywhere. Plain names only.
- Read this issue's existing metadata before pinning. Only update keys whose values changed.
- Do not create sub-issues, do not reassign any other issue, do not change any other issue's status.
- The created issue MUST land in `in_review` (when posted) or `cancelled` (green day) — never `done` (owner closes), never left in `todo`.

---
*Autopilot run triggered at 2026-05-27 04:00 UTC. After starting work, rename this issue to accurately reflect what you are doing.*

# ADR-001 — Default Tech Stack (Next.js + Supabase + Vercel)

**Status:** Adopted  
**Date:** 2026-05-26  
**Deciders:** Solution Architect (solution-architect), CEO Command Squad  
**Supersedes:** —  
**Superseded by:** —

---

## Decision

The devosnt App Factory adopts **Next.js + Supabase + Vercel** as its default production stack for all new business applications. This stack is used unless an app's requirements explicitly demand otherwise (see Reversal Conditions below). Every new app begins with this baseline; deviations require a written ADR from the Solution Architect approved by the CEO Command Squad before any build work starts.

---

## Layer-by-Layer Rationale

### Frontend — Next.js (App Router)
- Full-stack React framework with SSR, SSG, and API routes in one deployment unit.
- App Router enables per-route caching, streaming, and React Server Components — critical for dashboard-heavy business apps.
- Built-in image optimisation, i18n routing, and middleware — reduces need for bespoke infra.
- RTL/Arabic support is CSS-level; no framework constraint.
- Large ecosystem: Shadcn/ui, Radix, Tailwind — ships design systems fast.

### Backend / API — Next.js API Routes + Supabase Edge Functions
- API routes colocated with frontend eliminate a separate backend service for CRUD-heavy apps.
- Supabase Edge Functions (Deno) handle webhooks, background triggers, and heavy compute without a separate server.
- TypeScript end-to-end: shared types between frontend and backend; fewer runtime surprises.

### Database — Supabase (PostgreSQL)
- Postgres: the most mature open-source RDBMS; full ACID, JSON support, RLS, triggers, foreign keys.
- Row-Level Security (RLS) enforced at the DB layer — auth cannot be accidentally bypassed by a bad API route.
- Supabase wraps Postgres with an auto-generated REST + GraphQL API, reducing boilerplate for simple reads.
- Realtime subscriptions via Postgres logical replication — live dashboards and KDS screens without a separate message broker.
- Migrations via `supabase db diff` — version-controlled schema, reproducible across environments.
- Free tier for prototypes; predictable pricing for production.

### Auth — Supabase Auth (GoTrue)
- Supabase Auth is tightly coupled to the DB: `auth.uid()` is available inside RLS policies, making row-ownership trivial.
- Supports email/password, magic link, OAuth (Google, Apple, GitHub), and phone OTP out of the box.
- JWT access tokens with configurable expiry; refresh tokens stored server-side — no custom token infra needed.
- Custom claims via a `users` or `profiles` table + JWT hook for role-based access control (RBAC).

### Deploy — Vercel
- Git-push deploys: every PR gets a preview URL, main gets a production URL — zero DevOps overhead for most apps.
- Edge Network: assets and API routes served from PoPs closest to users; critical for Kuwait-based users hitting GCC-region Supabase.
- Environment variable management per environment (preview, staging, production) — no secrets in code.
- Serverless functions auto-scale; no capacity planning for spiky traffic.
- Integrates natively with Next.js: automatic ISR, image CDN, middleware edge runtime.

### Mobile — React Native + Expo (when required)
- Default is web-first (Next.js). When a native mobile app is required, React Native + Expo is the preferred choice.
- Shared business logic and API client code reused from the web project.
- Expo EAS Build handles iOS/Android CI without a Mac/Windows build machine.
- Not installed by default — a mobile sub-issue is created only when the PRD explicitly requires native mobile.

### Payments — MyFatoorah (Kuwait) / Stripe (International)
- MyFatoorah for KNet and local payment methods (GCC-regulated apps). Integrated via server-side API route.
- Stripe for international payments where KNet is not required; Stripe's SDK is best-in-class.
- Payment processing never in browser JS; always a Next.js API route or Supabase Edge Function.
- PCI scope minimised: payment form hosted by gateway (hosted fields / checkout); no card data touches our servers.

### Messaging / Notifications — Resend (email) + Twilio (SMS) + Supabase Realtime (in-app)
- Resend: transactional email with React Email templates; reliable deliverability, simple API.
- Twilio: SMS and WhatsApp for OTPs, alerts, and order notifications (Kuwait numbers supported).
- Supabase Realtime: in-app push via Postgres changes — no additional broker for live notification feeds.
- Push notifications (mobile): Expo Notifications + FCM/APNs when a native mobile layer exists.

### Analytics — PostHog (product analytics) + Supabase Views (operational reports)
- PostHog: open-source product analytics; self-hostable; captures events, funnels, session recordings, feature flags.
- Supabase Views + Materialized Views: operational KPIs (daily orders, revenue, inventory) queried directly from the DB — no ETL for simple reports.
- For heavy BI: Metabase connected to Supabase read replica (see data-warehouse-analytics skill for CDC pipeline).

---

## Alternatives Considered

| Layer | Alternative | Reason Not Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| Frontend | Remix | Smaller ecosystem; less tooling for our skill set; Vercel optimises Next.js preferentially |
| Frontend | Vue / Nuxt | Team and skills library are React-first; lower component ecosystem |
| Frontend | SvelteKit | Compelling but too niche for rapid onboarding of new agents/devs |
| Backend | Express.js / Fastify (standalone) | Adds a separate service to deploy and monitor; Next.js API routes cover most CRUD needs |
| Backend | NestJS | Overengineered for most apps; adds boilerplate without benefit at early scale |
| Database | Firebase Firestore | NoSQL means no RLS on relational data; schema drift is harder to audit; cost scales badly |
| Database | PlanetScale (MySQL) | No RLS; branching workflow adds complexity; Postgres ecosystem is richer |
| Database | MongoDB Atlas | Document model doesn't suit financial / relational business data |
| Auth | Auth0 | $$$; external JWT means no native `auth.uid()` in Supabase RLS; extra hop |
| Auth | Clerk | Good DX but same RLS coupling problem as Auth0; cost scales with MAU |
| Auth | Custom JWT | High maintenance; security risk; not justified for most apps |
| Deploy | AWS Amplify | More complex; less Next.js-native; slower preview deployments |
| Deploy | Railway / Render | Good for Node APIs but not optimised for Next.js App Router ISR/Edge |
| Deploy | Self-hosted k8s | Operational overhead far exceeds benefit at current scale |
| Payments | Tap Payments | Less documentation and SDK maturity vs MyFatoorah for KNet |
| Analytics | Mixpanel | Proprietary; cost at scale; PostHog is open-source with equivalent feature set |
| Analytics | Amplitude | Same tradeoff as Mixpanel |

---

## Reversal Conditions

This stack should be reconsidered (trigger a new ADR) when ANY of the following are true:

1. **Frontend:** An app requires a native desktop experience (Electron/Tauri), a game-like canvas-based UI, or a heavily SEO-independent SPA where bundle size is the dominant concern.
2. **Backend/API:** The app has stateful long-running processes (WebSockets for real-time gaming, complex job orchestration) that don't fit the serverless execution model.
3. **Database:** Throughput exceeds ~10 k writes/sec on a single Supabase project (time-series data, IoT streams), making a dedicated TSDB (TimescaleDB, ClickHouse) more appropriate.
4. **Auth:** A regulated industry requirement (banking, insurance) mandates on-premise identity or a specific certified IdP that Supabase Auth cannot integrate with.
5. **Deploy:** Monthly Vercel bill exceeds $2,000 for a single project (traffic or seat ceiling hit); evaluate self-hosted Next.js on Railway or containerised deploy.
6. **Mobile:** The app is primarily mobile (>80% usage) with heavy device API usage (BLE, NFC, offline-first) where Expo's managed workflow is insufficient and bare React Native or Flutter is required.
7. **Payments:** A geography outside GCC/International (e.g., India-first app) mandates a local gateway not supported by MyFatoorah or Stripe.
8. **Stack-wide:** A successor framework reaches maturity with measurably better DX + deployment economics and the team has evaluated it in a real build (not just a benchmark).

---

## Current Status

**Adopted** — in use for all devosnt App Factory builds as of 2026-05-26. This ADR is the baseline for monthly architecture reviews. Any proposed deviation must reference this document and state which reversal condition applies.

---

## Review Schedule

This ADR is reviewed quarterly (next: 2026-09-01) or when a reversal condition is triggered. The Solution Architect owns updates.

---

## Cross-References

- ADR-002 (DEV-37): App Hub Model — issue lifecycle and artifact organisation
- CLAUDE.md: `solution-architect` prompt, line: *"Default stack is Next.js + Supabase + Vercel unless the app demands otherwise"*
- Monthly Architecture Review: this ADR is the canonical stack baseline for all drift assessments
issues.title: ADR-001 — Default Tech Stack (Next.js + Supabase + Vercel)

## Input

Skill `parallel-issue-dispatcher` (ID: `9f4b229b-4f75-454b-9548-2df4e4b80916`) is registered and tested, but sprint-commander has not been configured to invoke it automatically after parent build plans are finalized.

## Expected Output
sprint-commander invokes `parallel-issue-dispatcher` automatically when a parent issue has ≥3 sub-issues in `backlog` with mixed dependencies. Until then, ceo or sprint-commander can call it manually.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] sprint-commander's instructions/skill list include `parallel-issue-dispatcher` as a default tool to consider after build-plan creation.
- [ ] First real dispatch validates Step 6 (re-run on sub-issue `done` cascades unblocking).
- [ ] Any defect found on first real use is filed as a follow-up against [DEV-98](mention://issue/2b595f8a-ea02-4677-b9a2-87f0edf2cb61).

## Dependencies

- [DEV-82](mention://issue/f5b0da68-4c0b-4edf-894a-1e0f146b4fda) — skill build (done)

## Risk
Low. Configuration change only; reversible.

## Definition of Done
sprint-commander references the skill in its operating instructions and has used it on at least one real parent issue. Step 6 cascade validated or follow-up filed.

## Skill Registration

**Multica Skill ID:** `035f76ed-3f96-42bd-91ef-152d11d359b2`
**Name:** `tdd-mastery`
**Source:** rohitg00/awesome-claude-code-toolkit → `skills/tdd-mastery/SKILL.md` (Apache 2.0)
**Imported by:** DEV-105

## Purpose
Red-Green-Refactor TDD cycle enforcement across all devosnt code tasks. Enforces 80% line / 75% branch coverage thresholds in CI. Pairs with `qa-test-plan-generator` at the QA gate.

## Stack Coverage
- **Primary:** TypeScript / Next.js (Vitest preferred, Jest fallback)
- **Secondary:** Python (pytest + coverage.py)
- **Tertiary:** Go (table-driven tests, go tool cover)

## Coverage Thresholds (wired into QA acceptance criteria)
- Line coverage: ≥ 80%
- Branch coverage: ≥ 75%
- Excludes: `*.generated.ts`, `*.d.ts`, `next.config.*`, `tailwind.config.*`, migration files

## Integration
- **qa-test-plan-generator**: Every checklist item from qa-test-plan-generator must map to a passing automated test or be explicitly noted as manual-only.
- **Build Squad**: Assumed active on any issue producing business logic, API routes, or data-mutation code.
- **QA Gate**: Coverage below threshold blocks `in_review → done`; triggers a `TEST:` sub-issue.

## Attribution
Apache 2.0 — original by rohitg00 (https://github.com/rohitg00/awesome-claude-code-toolkit)

## Improvement Rule
Review after 5 uses: count production bugs TDD would have caught, update language patterns, retire irrelevant anti-patterns.

## SECURITY REVIEW: sandbox-runtime import

**Dispatch sub-issue:** [DEV-117](mention://issue/9bdb8397-efee-4d72-ab46-cacde1b81ba8)
**Source URL:** https://github.com/anthropic-experimental/sandbox-runtime
**License:** Apache 2.0

### What to review
Anthropic's OS-level agent sandbox tool (`srt`). Restricts filesystem and network access on arbitrary processes using native OS primitives.

**Install footprint:**
- `npm install -g @anthropic-ai/sandbox-runtime`
- TypeScript/Node.js; no persistent daemon
- Invoked per-command: `srt "<command>"`

**Platform specifics (our environment):**
- **Linux (workspace):** Uses `bubblewrap` — requires `bwrap` binary and `user_namespaces` kernel feature
- **macOS:** Uses `sandbox-exec` (not relevant for workspace)
- **Network:** Proxy-based network filtering with configurable allowlist

**Required scopes / permissions:**
- Process execution with restricted namespaces (Linux user namespaces)
- No persistent disk writes beyond the sandboxed process
- No external API calls; purely local

**Specific areas to audit:**
1. Does bubblewrap require SUID or privileged capabilities in our Docker-based workspace?
2. Are the default allowlist defaults restrictive enough (which paths/hosts are allowed by default)?
3. Can the allowlist configuration be bypassed or injected by a malicious skill?
4. Supply chain audit: npm package `@anthropic-ai/sandbox-runtime` on npmjs.com — verify package provenance.
5. Are there known CVEs or privilege escalation paths in bubblewrap for our kernel version (6.8)?

**When done:**
- Pin `decision=green` or `decision=red` on this issue
- If green: run `multica issue status 9bdb8397-efee-4d72-ab46-cacde1b81ba8 todo` to promote the dispatch sub-issue
- If red: pin `blocked_reason=security_review_failed` on DEV-117 and leave it backlog
issues.title: SECURITY REVIEW: sandbox-runtime import

## SECURITY REVIEW: mcp-memory-service import

**Dispatch sub-issue:** [DEV-116](mention://issue/deb94651-8a6d-4630-bf8a-d833cca213ac)
**Source URL:** https://github.com/doobidoo/mcp-memory-service
**License:** Apache 2.0

### What to review
Python MCP server providing persistent agent memory via sqlite-vec + REST API.

**Install footprint:**
- `pip install mcp-memory-service`
- Runs as local daemon; optional Docker image
- Exposes HTTP REST API (configurable port) + MCP stdio transport

**Required scopes / permissions:**
- **Filesystem:** Reads/writes sqlite-vec database to local disk (configurable path)
- **Network:** Listens on a local HTTP port; no external outbound calls in core path
- **Background tasks:** Autonomy consolidation scheduler runs in background thread

**Specific areas to audit:**
1. Can the consolidation scheduler be triggered to write arbitrary data to disk?
2. Does the HTTP REST API expose authentication controls, or is it open by default?
3. Are there any supply chain risks in the PyPI package (`mcp-memory-service`)?
4. Does the knowledge graph traversal have injection risks?
5. Any OWASP-relevant patterns in the API surface (memory injection, data exfil via graph)?

**When done:**
- Pin `decision=green` or `decision=red` on this issue
- If green: run `multica issue status deb94651-8a6d-4630-bf8a-d833cca213ac todo` to promote the dispatch sub-issue
- If red: pin `blocked_reason=security_review_failed` on DEV-116 and leave it backlog

## SKILL IMPORT: sandbox-runtime

**Source:** https://github.com/anthropic-experimental/sandbox-runtime
**License:** Apache 2.0 (green)
**Stars:** 4,122 | **Last commit:** 2026-05-23 (from Anthropic experimental)

### What it does
Lightweight OS-level sandboxing tool (`srt`) for restricting filesystem and network access on arbitrary agent processes without requiring containers.
- macOS: uses `sandbox-exec`
- Linux: uses `bubblewrap`
- Network: proxy-based filtering with allowlist
- No container runtime needed

### Proposed Multica skill name
`sandbox-runtime-policy`

### PR-Ready Spec
- **Install:** `npm install -g @anthropic-ai/sandbox-runtime`
- **Usage:** Wrap shell invocations: `srt "<command>"`
- **Integration:** Hook into `ci-cd-pipeline-builder` and `agentic-codegen-loop` skills — apply `srt` when agent executes shell commands during builds
- **Skill shape:** Define a SKILL.md that specifies which commands require sandboxing, what filesystem paths to allow, and which network endpoints to permit

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `srt` installs successfully in workspace Linux environment (bubblewrap path)
- [ ] At least one build command runs sandboxed without breaking normal operation
- [ ] Allowlist defaults documented for Supabase CLI, npm, gh CLI
- [ ] Security review sub-issue returns `decision=green`

### Dependencies
- Security review sub-issue (sibling, assigned to security-auditor) must be `done` + `decision=green` before this issue is promoted to `todo`

### Risk
- Sandbox itself needs audit — review allowlist defaults and privilege escalation paths
- Linux bubblewrap requires specific kernel capabilities; may need adjustments in Docker-based workspace

### Definition of Done
- Sandbox policy documented in `sandbox-runtime-policy` SKILL.md
- Integration hooked into at least one existing build skill
- Security review cleared

# SKILL: multi-agent-coordinator

**Name:** multi-agent-coordinator  
**Model:** opus  
**Source:** [VoltAgent/awesome-claude-code-subagents — 09-meta-orchestration/multi-agent-coordinator.md](https://github.com/VoltAgent/awesome-claude-code-subagents) (MIT, security-cleared via DEV-63)  
**Version:** 1.0.0 — 2026-05-26

---

## Purpose

Coordinate concurrent agents working across multiple Multica issues — map dependency graphs onto parent/child issue hierarchies, prevent deadlock via the `blocked`-status workflow, route work through issue-based handoff, and apply fault tolerance aligned with the `agent-self-healing-policy` skill.

Use when a parent issue spawns three or more parallel sub-issues that have shared dependencies, ordering constraints, or synchronization requirements that cannot be resolved by a single assignee reading the issue description alone.

---

## Trigger

Invoke this skill when **any two** of the following are true for a work package:

1. Three or more parallel sub-issues exist under the same parent and at least one must wait for another to reach `in_review` or `done` before it can start.
2. Two or more squads own different pieces of the same feature and must exchange an artifact (schema, API contract, component) before one can unblock the other.
3. A previous parallel run caused a merge conflict, duplicate work, or a race condition on a shared resource (DB schema, env-var file, shared config).
4. The orchestrating agent (Sprint Commander or CEO Command Squad) explicitly delegates coordination to this skill.

---

## Required Input

| Field | Source | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Parent issue ID | Provided by caller | `MUL-4333e896` |
| List of sub-issue IDs and their titles | `multica issue list --parent <parent-id>` | `DEV-100`, `DEV-101`, `DEV-102` |
| Dependency declaration | Issue descriptions or explicit caller note | "DEV-101 requires DEV-100's schema to be in `in_review` first" |
| Squad/agent assignments per sub-issue | Issue metadata or assignee fields | `build-agent` on DEV-100, `db-agent` on DEV-101 |
| Shared resources (if any) | Caller or architecture docs | Supabase schema file, `.env.example`, shared API contract |

---

## Steps

### Phase 1 — Dependency Graph Construction

1. Read all sub-issues under the parent (`multica issue list --parent <parent-id> --output json`).
2. Parse each sub-issue's **Dependencies** field and comments for inter-issue blocking claims.
3. Build a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where each node is a sub-issue and each directed edge means "source must reach `in_review` or `done` before target can move to `todo`".
4. Run topological sort. If a cycle is detected, it is a **deadlock** — go to Phase 2 immediately.
5. Emit a plain-text dependency table in your first comment on the parent issue:

```
Dependency order (topological):
  [start] DEV-100 (no deps)
  [start] DEV-103 (no deps)
  [wait]  DEV-101 → unblocks when DEV-100 reaches in_review
  [wait]  DEV-102 → unblocks when DEV-101 reaches done
```

6. Set `--status todo` only for issues with no incoming edges. Set `--status backlog` for all others.

### Phase 2 — Deadlock Detection and Prevention

A **deadlock** in Multica terms is any cycle in the dependency graph (A waits for B, B waits for A), or a situation where two agents each hold a shared resource lock and both are waiting for the other's resource.

Detection:
- Topological sort failure → structural deadlock.
- Two issues both in `blocked` status citing each other as the blocker → runtime deadlock.

Resolution:
1. Identify the minimum edge to cut from the cycle (prefer cutting the dependency with the least work remaining).
2. Create a new sub-issue that produces the shared artifact independently (no other dependencies).
3. Re-route both blocked issues to depend on this new sub-issue instead of each other.
4. Set both originally-blocked issues to `backlog`; set the new resolver issue to `todo`.
5. Post a comment on the parent explaining the restructuring.

Prevention rules (enforce these when creating sub-issues):
- Never create two issues that each list the other in their **Dependencies** field.
- Shared resources (schema files, env files, API contracts) must be owned by exactly one issue at a time; all consumers list that issue as a dependency, not each other.
- If the dependency chain is longer than 4 hops, escalate to the CEO Command Squad before proceeding.

### Phase 3 — Message Routing (Issue-Based Handoff)

Multica's handoff primitive is a sub-issue + assignee mention, not a direct message. Route work as follows:

| Scenario | Action |
|---|---|
| Agent A's output is needed by Agent B | When A reaches `in_review`, add a comment on B's issue: "DEV-100 is now `in_review`. Artifact: `<url or description>`. You may proceed." Then promote B to `todo`. |
| Agent A produces an artifact consumed by multiple agents | Post the artifact link on the parent issue (not each child) so all consumers see it in one canonical place. |
| Broadcast (all agents need to know something) | Comment on the parent issue; do not mention every agent individually — they will re-read on their next trigger. |
| Escalation to a human | Use `[@Name](mention://member/<user-id>)` on the relevant issue. One mention per escalation; no follow-up mention unless the human has not responded after the stated SLA. |
| First-touch delegation to an agent | Use `[@AgentName](mention://agent/<agent-id>)` in the first delegation comment only. Never re-mention the same agent to acknowledge or thank — that triggers a loop. |

### Phase 4 — Fault Tolerance and Recovery

Aligned with the `agent-self-healing-policy` skill:

| Failure Mode | Detection | Recovery |
|---|---|---|
| Agent run fails (non-zero exit, timeout) | `agent-self-healing-policy` triggers auto-retry on a different model | Coordinator re-reads the issue after 10 minutes; if still `in_progress` with no new comment, escalate to CEO Command Squad |
| Agent posts `blocked` status | Issue status = `blocked`, comment present | Read the `blocked_reason` metadata or comment; resolve the blocker or create a resolver sub-issue (Phase 2); re-promote to `todo` |
| Agent produces wrong artifact (QA rejects) | Issue moved back from `in_review` to `in_progress` | Re-read the QA rejection comment; create a correction sub-issue if the scope is more than a 1-hour fix; otherwise reassign in-place |
| Shared resource race (two agents edit same file) | Merge conflict in PR | Whichever PR was merged second owns resolution; create a cleanup sub-issue assigned to the same agent |
| Coordinator self-failure (this skill crashes) | Parent issue stuck in `in_progress` with no comment update for > 2 h | CEO Command Squad re-triggers this skill on the parent issue from scratch; all sub-issue states are re-read as ground truth |

Circuit-breaker rule: If three consecutive recovery attempts on the same sub-issue fail, set that issue to `blocked`, pin `blocked_reason = "3 consecutive recovery failures — human review required"` in metadata, and escalate to the workspace owner.

### Phase 5 — Parallel Execution Governance

Enforce these invariants throughout the coordination lifecycle:

1. **One assignee per issue.** Never assign two agents to the same issue simultaneously.
2. **Shared resources serialized.** Issues that write to the same file or DB table must be in a serial dependency chain, not parallel.
3. **Status discipline.** An issue moves `todo → in_progress → in_review → done`. Backwards moves (`in_review → in_progress`) are valid (QA rejection); skipping steps is not.
4. **Parent stays open.** The parent issue does not move to `done` until every child is `done` or `cancelled` with a documented reason.
5. **No ghost work.** If a sub-issue becomes unnecessary after another issue completes (e.g., a dependency produced a superset), cancel it with `multica issue status <id> cancelled` and post a comment explaining why.

### Phase 6 — Monitoring and Completion

Poll the parent issue's children on each re-trigger:

```
For each child issue:
  if status == done or cancelled: mark as resolved
  if status == blocked: apply Phase 4 recovery
  if status == in_review and dependents exist: promote dependents to todo (Phase 3)
  if status == in_progress and last_comment > 2h ago: flag for health check

If all children are done or cancelled:
  post final summary comment on parent (see Output section)
  move parent to in_review
```

---

## Output

### Per-run comment on the parent issue

```
**Coordination status — <timestamp>**

Resolved: DEV-100 (done), DEV-103 (done)
In progress: DEV-101 (in_progress, build-agent, no blockers)
Waiting: DEV-102 (backlog, unblocks when DEV-101 reaches in_review)
Blocked: none

Next actions:
- DEV-101 expected in_review by EOD; DEV-102 will be promoted then.
- No deadlocks detected.
```

### Final summary comment when all children complete

```
**Multi-agent coordination complete — <parent-issue-id>**

Total sub-issues: N
  Completed: X
  Cancelled: Y (reasons documented on each issue)
  Escalations: Z (list)

Shared resources: <list of files/tables touched and who owns the final state>
Duration: <start → end>
Lessons / patterns to extract: <any reusable coordination pattern observed>
```

---

## Quality Checklist

Before closing out any coordination run, verify:

- [ ] Every sub-issue is in `done`, `cancelled` (with reason), or actively progressing with no stale blockers.
- [ ] No two issues are simultaneously in `in_progress` writing to the same shared resource.
- [ ] No cycle exists in the current dependency graph.
- [ ] Every `blocked` issue has a `blocked_reason` comment and a recovery action assigned.
- [ ] The parent issue has an up-to-date coordination status comment.
- [ ] No agent has been mentioned more than once for the same delegation (loop-prevention).
- [ ] All cancelled issues have a documented reason in the issue comments.
- [ ] Escalations to CEO Command Squad or workspace owner are documented on the parent.

---

## Failure Handling

| Failure | Immediate action |
|---|---|
| Cannot list sub-issues (CLI error) | Retry once; if fails again, post `blocked` on parent with `blocked_reason = "multica CLI unavailable"` and page workspace owner |
| Cycle detected in dependency graph | Apply Phase 2 restructuring; if cycle persists after restructuring, escalate to CEO Command Squad |
| Three recovery failures on one issue | Set `blocked`, pin metadata, escalate to workspace owner |
| Coordination comment fails to post | Retry with `--content-file` flag using a temp file path |
| Dependency chain > 4 hops | Escalate to CEO Command Squad before proceeding; do not auto-restructure deep chains |

---

## Improvement Rule

After every 5 uses of this skill, the Skill Builder agent reviews:

1. Were any dependency cycles detected? What structural pattern caused them?
2. Were the topological-sort phase outputs accurate enough for agents to act without re-reading this skill?
3. Did the fault-tolerance phase catch all failures within the 2-hour window?
4. Did any mention cause an unintended agent loop? Tighten the routing rules if so.
5. Update this skill file with refined rules; bump the version field; post the revision notes on the skill's library issue.

Retire this skill if Multica adds native dependency tracking between issues — at that point the DAG construction phase becomes redundant.

---

## Coordination Patterns Reference (Adapted to Multica)

| Pattern | Multica implementation |
|---|---|
| Master-worker | One parent issue (master) with N child issues (workers); coordinator promotes children sequentially or in parallel per DAG |
| Pipeline | Serial chain: each child's `in_review` status triggers the next child's `todo` promotion |
| Scatter-gather | All leaves set to `todo` simultaneously; coordinator waits for all to reach `done`; posts aggregated result on parent |
| Publish-subscribe | Shared artifact posted as comment on parent; each consuming agent reads from parent comment, not from the producer's issue |
| Circuit breaker | Three failures → `blocked` + escalate; no further retries until human clears the block |
| Fork-join | Parent forks at issue creation (all children `todo`); joins when all children reach `done` and coordinator posts final summary |

# COMPETITIVE BRIEF: LOVABLE
**Run date:** 2026-05-26

## What They Do
Lovable (lovable.dev) is an AI-powered full-stack app builder that generates production-ready React/TypeScript/Supabase applications from plain English prompts, eliminating the need to write code or configure visual builders. The platform targets non-technical founders, startup teams, and product managers who need a working web app for validation or MVP launch quickly. Its core value proposition is delivering a complete, deployable system — frontend UI, database schema, authentication, and payment integrations — in hours rather than weeks, with full GitHub code export so the user owns the output.

## Onboarding Flow
1. **Landing page chatbot** — Visitors describe an app idea and see live AI output *before* creating an account, proving capability with zero commitment.
2. **Email-only signup** — Five-second registration; no password required, no verification delay.
3. **Personalization survey** — Minimal discovery questions to tailor output; no template picker.
4. **Guided integration prompts** — Supabase (DB), Stripe (payments), and OAuth providers are introduced in context as each becomes relevant, not all at once.
5. **Real-time task narration** — The builder narrates AI subtasks ("Create schema → Compose UI → Spin preview") with an inline credit counter visible throughout.

Notable UX choices: share buttons are public-by-default to encourage viral loops; credit remaining is surfaced inline to avoid surprise bills.

## Pricing (as of 2026-05-26)
| Tier | Price | Credits/month | Notable limits |
|---|---|---|---|
| Free | $0 | 30 (5/day cap) | 5 lovable.app subdomains, private projects |
| Pro | $25/mo | 100 | Custom domains, unlimited collaborators |
| Business | $50/mo | 100 | SSO, team workspace, design templates, opt-out of data training |
| Enterprise | Custom | Custom | Volume pricing, SCIM, dedicated support |

Credit consumption varies by complexity: simple styling ~0.5 credits; a full landing page 2+ credits. Every prompt (including AI-introduced bug fixes) consumes credits. Student discount: up to 50% off Pro.

## Strengths
1. **Full-stack generation with code ownership** — GitHub two-way sync exports standard React/TypeScript/Tailwind code; users can deploy anywhere and fork freely after export. ([Lovable FAQ](https://lovable.dev/faq/code-export), [Export Guide](https://www.alanbagi.com/blog/can-i-export-an-lovable-project-guide))
2. **Auto-generated Supabase schema + RLS** — Plain-English prompts produce SQL schemas, table relationships, and Row-Level Security policies simultaneously with UI bindings and real-time subscriptions, eliminating manual backend setup. ([Lovable Supabase Integration](https://uibakery.io/blog/lovable-supabase-integration), [Docs](https://docs.lovable.dev/integrations/supabase))
3. **Agentic mode with 91% fewer errors** — Agent Mode makes multi-file changes and validates them with built-in browser testing and edge function validation before delivery. ([Lovable Blog](https://lovable.dev/blog/setting-the-standard-for-agentic-development), [Docs](https://docs.lovable.dev/features/agent-mode))

## Weaknesses
1. **Credit trap on AI-introduced bugs** — Every prompt costs credits, including re-fixes of bugs the AI introduced. Users report 70% of spend goes to fixing AI mistakes; costs of $200–$3,000+ for moderately complex apps are documented. The AI lacks persistent cross-session context, causing fixed bugs to resurface.
2. **Security vulnerabilities in generated code** — February 2026: 16 vulnerabilities (6 critical) found in a single Lovable-hosted app exposing 18,000 users' data. A 2026 audit found 10%+ of sampled production apps with live vulnerabilities. Generated code lacks rate limiting, CSRF tokens, and proper secrets management. ([The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/02/27/lovable_app_vulnerabilities/))
3. **Scaling failures past MVP** — Multi-step workflows and complex business logic confuse the AI. Data structures are tightly coupled and break when requirements change. Code that works in Lovable fails locally due to sync/build issues. Not suitable for enterprise or long-term applications.

## Patterns to Copy
1. **Value-before-signup chatbot** — Visitors see working AI output on the landing page before creating an account, proving capability and pre-qualifying intent. | Implementation note: build a public "describe your app" demo that generates a mockup or screen list without requiring login; capture emails at the output step.
2. **Transparent inline cost display** — Credits remaining are visible contextually during every action, not buried in a settings page. | Implementation note: surface usage/quota inline wherever a user takes a resource-consuming action; frame it as "X credits remaining" not as a warning.
3. **Agentic task narration UI** — Real-time narration of AI subtasks ("Designing schema → Wiring UI → Running tests") maintains psychological momentum on slow operations. | Implementation note: stream agent step labels into the UI for any long-running build or generation task; a spinner alone is insufficient.

## Patterns to Beat
1. **Per-message credit burn with no refund on AI failures** — Lovable charges credits for every prompt regardless of outcome, trapping users in expensive debug loops. | Our differentiation: charge credits only at verifiable checkpoints (compilation, test pass, deploy-ready). Auto-refund or credit-reverse when the AI re-introduces a previously-fixed bug (detected via diff comparison).
2. **Stateless agent with no cross-project memory** — Lovable's Agent Mode processes each session in isolation; design systems and naming conventions must be re-explained for every new project. | Our differentiation: maintain a persistent workspace memory layer that learns project conventions, component libraries, and schema patterns across all user projects and applies them proactively to new builds.
3. **No security hardening by default** — Generated apps ship without rate limiting, input validation, audit logging, or proper secrets management. | Our differentiation: auto-inject a security scaffold (helmet.js / equivalent, input sanitisation, secrets via env var, audit log stub) on every generated backend; run an automated pre-deploy security report with a one-click "fix all" option.

## Decision
**Study only** — Lovable has strong UX patterns worth adopting (value-before-signup, transparent costs, agentic narration) but its credit-trap pricing, documented security failures, and scaling ceiling make it a cautionary example rather than a source to import or adapt directly; our differentiation should explicitly solve the problems that cause Lovable users to outgrow the platform.

## Sources
- [Lovable FAQ — Code Export](https://lovable.dev/faq/code-export)
- [Lovable Agent Mode Docs](https://docs.lovable.dev/features/agent-mode)
- [Lovable Agent Blog](https://lovable.dev/blog/setting-the-standard-for-agentic-development)
- [Lovable Supabase Integration](https://uibakery.io/blog/lovable-supabase-integration)
- [Lovable Pricing 2026](https://flowith.io/blog/lovable-pricing-2026-free-vs-starter-vs-pro/)
- [Lovable Pricing & Credits](https://www.banani.co/blog/lovable-pricing-and-credits)
- [Lovable Onboarding Teardown](https://www.candu.ai/blog/lovables-agentic-onboarding)
- [Lovable App Exposed 18K Users — The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/02/27/lovable_app_vulnerabilities/)
- [Lovable Bugs & Realistic Costs 2026](https://forasoft.medium.com/lovable-app-bugs-how-to-fix-them-when-to-hire-developers-realistic-costs-2026-946199c6496d)
- [Lovable Limitations Guide](https://p0stman.com/guides/lovable-limitations)
- [Why Startups Outgrow Lovable](https://www.fastdev.com/blog/blog/startups-scaleups-lovable-limitations/)
- [Lovable Review 2026](https://weavai.app/blog/en/2026/04/26/2026-lovable-review-is-this-ai-app-builder-worth-it/)

## Skill ID
`7a01483c-d432-4a1e-80f6-4bdab8052430`

## Purpose
OWASP-aware code review covering logic errors, vulnerability patterns, complexity analysis, and best practices. Acts as the first gate before `security-review-checklist` on the `in_review → done` transition in the App Factory workflow. Run on Opus.

## Source
Adapted from VoltAgent/awesome-claude-code-subagents `04-quality-security/code-reviewer.md` (MIT, 2026-05-25). Security clearance: DEV-63 PASS.

## Trigger
- Any squad moves an issue to `in_review` for user-facing or API code.
- Runs before `security-review-checklist` — PASS here is a prerequisite.

## Required Input
- PR diff or GitHub PR link.
- Stack context: Next.js (App Router), Supabase (Postgres + RLS), TypeScript.
- Permission matrix from the issue or PRD.

## Key Features
- **OWASP Top-10 table** mapped to Next.js/Supabase patterns (A01–A10).
- **Logic & Correctness**: async paths, hooks, server-side filtering.
- **Complexity**: flags functions > 50 lines, cyclomatic complexity > 10.
- **Best Practices**: Server Components, zod validation, RLS policies, i18n/RTL, a11y.
- **Bash tools**: user-permission-gated only (npm audit, tsc, eslint). No auto-execution.

## Integration
`code-reviewer` (PASS) → `security-review-checklist` (PASS) → `in_review → done` allowed.

## Quality Checklist
- [x] Skill registered in workspace
- [x] Bash permission noted with user-gating requirement documented
- [x] OWASP top-10 checks mapped to devosnt stack (Next.js/Supabase)
- [x] Quality checklist and failure handling defined
- [x] Wired into security-review-checklist gate as pre-requisite

## Improvement Rule
After every 5 runs: review FAIL patterns. Retire checks that never fire after 10 consecutive PASS runs.

## Input
Workspace Upgrade Report — 2026-05-26, action #3. 32 issues currently sit in `status=in_review` while quality-gate-manager (id: d87c831d-bd4d-459f-9672-d633e238c44f) has WIP=0. The CEO prompt never delegates the `in_review → done` gate to quality-gate-manager — every in_review issue waits for an ad-hoc CEO review that never fires.

Concrete sample of the stuck queue (full list in the report comment on the parent issue): DEV-1, DEV-25, DEV-35–DEV-89 in_review subset.

## Expected Output
A patch to the CEO agent's `instructions` (a.k.a. system prompt) that adds an explicit `in_review` sweep step:

- On every CEO run, after routing new App Requests, **scan `multica issue list --status in_review`** for issues NOT assigned to ceo where (a) updated_at > 12h ago OR (b) DoD checklist is present in the description.
- For each such issue, create exactly one sub-issue assigned to quality-gate-manager with the verify-and-decide prompt (PASS → flip parent to done; VETO → flip parent to backlog with a `blocked_reason`).
- Cap dispatch at 5 in_review reviews per CEO run to avoid flood.
- Skip DEV-36, skip any issue whose assignee is ceo (those are escalations, not shippable work).
- Never `@mention` quality-gate-manager — assignment fires the trigger.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CEO instructions updated via `multica agent update d9f942b9-e245-4e28-99eb-8f12fbebc2c3 --instructions-file <patched.md>` (or your standard prompt-optimizer flow).
- [ ] Diff posted as a comment on this issue with before/after of the new section.
- [ ] Dry-run test: simulate the new prompt against the current 32-issue in_review pile and confirm the first 5 picks are sensible (post the picks as a comment).

## Dependencies
None hard. Independent of skill-builder / memory-curator actions.

## Risk
Medium. Wrong selection could close a not-actually-done issue. Mitigations:
- The new sub-issue does NOT auto-close the parent — it delegates the decision to quality-gate-manager.
- Cap of 5/run gives CEO a chance to course-correct week-over-week.
- DEV-36 and ceo-assigned escalations are excluded.

## Definition of Done
- CEO prompt patched.
- Diff + dry-run picks posted as a comment.
- The next weekly Workspace Upgrade run should observe in_review count trending down — note the baseline (32) so next week's loop can diff it.

2. `figma-to-ux-spec` (id: 3b768b92-e4b2-47f1-9172-3bc20eeb7695)

Several of these are referenced by name in active autopilot prompts (DEV-67/DEV-82/DEV-83/DEV-84) but no agent can actually invoke them.

## Expected Output
Each orphan skill is attached to the right agent (or a small set), via `multica agent update <id> --skill <skill-id>` (or whatever the current CLI verb is — check `multica agent --help`). Suggested mapping:

## Purpose

`fe3ddde3-3e97-48a4-a9ad-91806868224b`

- Feature reaches `in_review` with user-visible code or data access.
- Pre-deploy gate requested by CEO Command Squad or QA Security Squad.

- Compliance attestation needed before customer delivery.
- Post-mortem root-cause security analysis.

## Integration with existing skills
- Runs **before** `security-review-checklist` (deep audit vs fast gate).
- Feeds findings to `audit-log-designer` on logging gaps.
- Coordinates with `nextjs-supabase-builder` on RLS templates.

## Improvement Rule
Review after every 5 uses; add Grep patterns for any post-audit discovered vulnerabilities; retire consistently N/A items.

## Source
VoltAgent/awesome-claude-code-subagents, MIT licence, reviewed and cleared in DEV-63.

## Rationale
The workspace CLAUDE.md declares a default stack (Next.js + Supabase + Vercel) but there is no ADR documenting WHY this stack was chosen, what alternatives were considered, and under what conditions it should be changed. ADR-002 (DEV-37) exists for the App Hub Model but references a decision (the default stack) that has no ADR. Monthly architecture reviews cannot reliably assess stack drift without a baseline ADR-001.

## Evidence
- Only ADR found: DEV-37 (ADR-002)
- CLAUDE.md states: 'Default stack is Next.js + Supabase + Vercel unless the app demands otherwise'
- No ADR-001 issue exists in '00 - Factory Brain' project
- Monthly arch review window: 2026-04-01 → 2026-05-26

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] ADR-001 issue created in '00 - Factory Brain' project with title 'ADR-001 — Default Tech Stack (Next.js + Supabase + Vercel)'
- [ ] ADR-001 body includes: decision (one paragraph), layer-by-layer rationale, alternatives considered, reversal conditions (when would we migrate away?), current status (adopted)
- [ ] Layers covered: frontend, backend/API, DB, auth, deploy, mobile, payments, messaging, analytics
- [ ] ADR-001 cross-referenced in CLAUDE.md or the solution-architect prompt so future runs cite it

## Dependencies
- None (standalone document)

## Risk
Without ADR-001, every future monthly review starts without a documented baseline; stack drift is invisible and reversal decisions are made without documented rationale.

## Definition of Done
ADR-001 issue exists, is in 'done' status, and is cross-referenced in the architecture review process.

## Effort: S (half day)

issues.description:
## Rationale
CVE-2026-46701 (HIGH — Network-AI: Unauthenticated Cross-Origin MCP Tool Invocation via Empty Default Secret) and CVE-2026-46519 (HIGH — MCP Server Kubernetes: Tool Access Control Bypass via Presentation-Layer Filtering Without Execution-Layer Enforcement) demonstrate that MCP servers with empty or absent secrets allow unauthenticated cross-origin tool invocations. ADR-002 (DEV-37) plans to expose the codegraph layer via MCP (`codegraph_search`, `codegraph_trace`, `codegraph_callers`, `codegraph_impact`). If MCP servers are stood up without execution-layer auth, any cross-tenant or unauthenticated call can invoke codegraph tools.

## Evidence
- CVE-2026-46701: https://github.com/advisories (GitHub Security Advisory — npm ecosystem)
- CVE-2026-46519: https://github.com/advisories (GitHub Security Advisory — npm ecosystem)
- ADR-002 (DEV-37): codegraph MCP integration planned

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A security policy document (or CLAUDE.md addendum) is written specifying: all MCP servers deployed in this workspace MUST have a non-empty shared secret configured; execution-layer auth (not just presentation-layer filtering) must be enforced
- [ ] Policy covers: secret rotation cadence, how to validate auth on each MCP server tool call, logging of rejected attempts
- [ ] Policy is referenced in the `app-hub-bootstrap` skill so every new app that adds MCP tools gets the check automatically
- [ ] Security review sign-off before any codegraph MCP server goes live

## Dependencies
- ADR-002 (DEV-37) codegraph integration
- Workspace CLAUDE.md update rights

## Risk
Unauthenticated cross-origin tool invocation when codegraph MCP goes live; potential data leakage across app tenants.

## Definition of Done
Security policy written and referenced in app-hub-bootstrap skill. No MCP server deployment proceeds without this policy in place.

## Effort: S (1-2 days)
issues.title: ARCH ACTION: Add MCP server auth policy before codegraph integration
